rationale: The citing paper uses Baumgartner and Steenkamp (2001) repeatedly for core conceptual and methodological setup, not just background listing. It adopts that paper's response-style framing (e.g., acquiescence/noncontingent responding), uses its acquiescence operationalizations (ARS-style measures), and references its correction approach when discussing analytical remedies. The cited work also informs the paper's positioning about reversed-item design and contamination from response styles, which the new study then contrasts with item verification difficulty.
relation_description: Swain et al. treat Baumgartner and Steenkamp (2001) as a key foundation for defining and measuring response-style contamination, especially acquiescence-related diagnostics and adjustments. They build from that foundation to test whether misresponse to reversed Likert items is instead primarily driven by item verification difficulty.
